title: A ChildпїЅs Pity
content:
В В В В No sweeter thing than childrenпїЅs ways and wiles,
В В В В Surely, we say, can gladden eyes and ears:
В В В В Yet sometime sweeter than their words or smiles
В В В В Are even their tears.

В В В В To one for once a piteous tale was read,
В В В В How, when the murderous mother crocodile
В В В В Was slain, her fierce brood famished, and lay dead,
В В В В Starved, by the Nile.

В В В В In vast green reed-beds on the vast grey slime
В В В В Those monsters motherless and helpless lay,
В В В В Perishing only for the parentпїЅs crime
В В В В Whose seed were they.

В В В В Hours after, toward the dusk, our blithe small bird
В В В В Of Paradise, who has our hearts in keeping,
В В В В Was heard or seen, but hardly seen or heard,
В В В В For pity weeping.

В В В В He was so sorry, sitting still apart,
В В В В For the poor little crocodiles, he said.
В В В В Six years had given him, for an angelпїЅs heart,
В В В В A childпїЅs instead.

В В В В Feigned tears the false beasts shed for murderous ends,
В В В В We know from travellersпїЅ tales of crocodiles:
В В В В But these tears wept upon them of my friendпїЅs
В В В В Outshine his smiles.

В В В В What heavenliest angels of what heavenly city
В В В В Could match the heavenly heart in children here?
В В В В The heart that hallowing all things with its pity
В В В В Casts out all fear?

В В В В So lovely, so divine, so dear their laughter
В В В В Seems to us, we know not what could be more dear:
В В В В But lovelier yet we see the sign thereafter
В В В В Of such a tear.

В В В В With sense of love half laughing and half weeping
В В В В We met your tears, our small sweet-spirited friend:
В В В В Let your love have us in its heavenly keeping
В В В В To lifeпїЅs last end.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Algernon Charles Swinburne represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Algernon Charles Swinburne's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
